CONCEPT 06

Online context mixing

The PAQ/cmix reference ceiling: cheap order-k models + online logistic mixing.

a reference, not a candidate

This page describes a yardstick — the bpb-per-FLOP that single-pass online learning can reach — not an entrant in the search. It tells us what a real candidate should try to approach.

Intuition

Our metric is the Hutter Prize: bits-per-byte on enwik8. And the best results there are not trained transformers — they are online context-mixing compressors (PAQ / cmix / nncp). Their recipe is almost embarrassingly simple:

  1. Keep a handful of cheap specialists, each a different “if you only looked at this much context, what comes next?” model — order-0 (global byte frequency), order-1 (given the last byte), order-2, order-3 (given the last 2–3 bytes).
  2. Mix their predictions into one distribution.
  3. Learn the mixing weights online, in a single streaming pass — no pretraining, no backprop through a deep net. Every byte: predict, get graded, nudge the weights, fold the byte into the counts, move on.

Because it learns entirely while reading (transductive), it is a pure expression of Source-(iv): lots of loss-reduction per FLOP. It carries zero pretrained parameters and still reaches far lower bpb than an untrained transformer at a tiny fraction of the compute.

The math

Each specialist is a smoothed conditional frequency table. For order-kkk, with the last kkk bytes as context, let ck[b]c_k[b]ck​[b] be how many times byte bbb has followed that context. Add-α\alphaα (Laplace) smoothing turns counts into a probability (V=256V = 256V=256):

pk[b]=ck[b]+α∑b′ck[b′]+αVp_k[b] = \frac{c_k[b] + \alpha}{\sum_{b'} c_k[b'] + \alpha V}pk​[b]=∑b′​ck​[b′]+αVck​[b]+α​

An unseen context (all-zero counts) gives the uniform distribution — the specialist abstains.

Logistic mixing. Stretch each prediction into log-space, take a weighted sum, and squash back with softmax — a weighted product of experts:

sk[b]=log⁡pk[b],z[b]=∑kwk sk[b],P[b]=softmax(z)[b]s_k[b] = \log p_k[b], \qquad z[b] = \sum_k w_k\, s_k[b], \qquad P[b] = \mathrm{softmax}(z)[b]sk​[b]=logpk​[b],z[b]=∑k​wk​sk​[b],P[b]=softmax(z)[b]

Online weight learning. The mixer is one-layer multinomial logistic regression on the features sks_ksk​. After byte yyy is revealed, take one SGD step on the cross-entropy L=−log⁡P[y]L = -\log P[y]L=−logP[y]. The softmax-CE gradient is clean and hand-checkable:

∂L∂wk=∑b(P[b]−1{b=y}) sk[b],wk←wk−η ∂L∂wk\frac{\partial L}{\partial w_k} = \sum_b \big(P[b] - \mathbb{1}\{b = y\}\big)\, s_k[b], \qquad w_k \leftarrow w_k - \eta\, \frac{\partial L}{\partial w_k}∂wk​∂L​=∑b​(P[b]−1{b=y})sk​[b],wk​←wk​−η∂wk​∂L​

Then the byte is folded into every order-kkk count table (ck[y]+=1c_k[y]\mathrel{+}=1ck​[y]+=1), so the next prediction is already a little better. That is the entire learning algorithm.

Run the mixer

recent bytes context
→
order-k counts smoothed p_k
→
stretch + mix z = Σ w_k log p_k
→
softmax P(next byte)
↶ after reveal: SGD step on w + counts[y] += 1 (single online pass)

FLOP honesty

This mechanism’s dominant compute is not matmuls — it is table lookups, count updates, and the logistic mix/update, all O(K⋅V)O(K \cdot V)O(K⋅V) per byte. The shared FLOP counter omits elementwise work for the transformer only because matmuls dominate there; that omission is conditional. So every per-byte operation here is charged through the non-matmul pointwise / gather primitives, and each step charges exactly the branches it actually runs — not a constant per-byte estimate (which would over-charge early bytes and abstaining specialists).

read honestly

With K=4K = 4K=4 predictors and V=256V = 256V=256, a fully-warmed byte costs ≈9.5k\approx 9.5\text{k}≈9.5k FLOPs — small, but counted to the FLOP. Leaving it uncharged would let the reference look artificially free; the whole point of the primitives is that it does not.

Worked example

Two specialists, a 2-symbol alphabet, weights w=[0.5,0.5]w = [0.5, 0.5]w=[0.5,0.5], stretched inputs (already log⁡p\log plogp) s=[[1,3],[5,7]]s = [[1, 3], [5, 7]]s=[[1,3],[5,7]].

  • Mix: z=0.5⋅[1,3]+0.5⋅[5,7]=[3,5]z = 0.5\cdot[1,3] + 0.5\cdot[5,7] = [3, 5]z=0.5⋅[1,3]+0.5⋅[5,7]=[3,5].
  • Squash: P=softmax([3,5])=[e−2,1]/(1+e−2)≈[0.119,0.881]P = \mathrm{softmax}([3,5]) = [e^{-2}, 1]/(1 + e^{-2}) \approx [0.119, 0.881]P=softmax([3,5])=[e−2,1]/(1+e−2)≈[0.119,0.881].

The true byte is 000. The error is P−onehot(0)=[−0.881,0.881]P - \text{onehot}(0) = [-0.881, 0.881]P−onehot(0)=[−0.881,0.881], so the gradient per specialist is gk=∑berr[b]⋅sk[b]g_k = \sum_b \text{err}[b]\cdot s_k[b]gk​=∑b​err[b]⋅sk​[b]:

g0=(−0.881)(1)+(0.881)(3)=1.76,g1=(−0.881)(5)+(0.881)(7)=1.76g_0 = (-0.881)(1) + (0.881)(3) = 1.76, \qquad g_1 = (-0.881)(5) + (0.881)(7) = 1.76g0​=(−0.881)(1)+(0.881)(3)=1.76,g1​=(−0.881)(5)+(0.881)(7)=1.76

With η=0.1\eta = 0.1η=0.1: w←[0.5,0.5]−0.1⋅[1.76,1.76]=[0.324,0.324]w \leftarrow [0.5, 0.5] - 0.1\cdot[1.76, 1.76] = [0.324, 0.324]w←[0.5,0.5]−0.1⋅[1.76,1.76]=[0.324,0.324]. Both weights shrink because both specialists put more mass on byte 111 than the truth warranted — the mixer learns to trust sharp-but-wrong inputs less.

What the curve says

On the bundled English sample, adding orders walks the bpb-vs-FLOP frontier down (4.71 → 4.17 bpb as the per-byte FLOPs roughly triple) while an untrained transformer sits at ≈8.0\approx 8.0≈8.0 bpb spending ∼\sim∼450× more compute. That gap is the ceiling a candidate is chasing — see the context-mixing reference experiment.

  • Compression = prediction — why a compressor is a predictor.
